title: VaultGuard — Privilege Escalation Chain
description: VaultGuard is a cybersecurity firm's internal portal for managing penetration test reports and team operations. No single vulnerability is critical — but chaining several low-severity issues together could give you access to the classified vault. Can you escalate your way to the top?
objectives: Create an account on the portal and explore the available features.
Find information about other team members that you shouldn't have access to.
Discover a credential that belongs to a privileged user.
Access the restricted vault and retrieve the classified flag.
hints: The Team page only shows your own profile in detail, but the underlying API might be more permissive. Check the API docs.
Try fetching /api/team/1, /api/team/2, etc. The CTO's profile has something extra that other profiles don't.
The API documentation page mentions a vault endpoint that requires an API key in the X-Api-Key header.
Fetch /api/team/1 to get the CTO's apiKey field, then use curl or the browser console: fetch("/api/admin/vault", {headers:{"X-Api-Key":"<key>"}}).then(r=>r.json()).then(console.log)

title: PawsVault — Second-Order SQL Injection
description: PawsVault is an internal portal for a veterinary clinic where pet owners manage pet records, appointments, and prescriptions. The application uses parameterized queries for registration and login — but something dangerous lurks deeper. A username stored in the database is later used unsafely in other queries. Can you exploit this second-order SQL injection to access confidential admin data?
objectives: Create a new account on the portal and explore the dashboard, pets, appointments, and prescriptions features.
Notice that the dashboard loads user-specific data based on your stored username.
Register with a carefully crafted username that will manipulate a downstream SQL query when loaded from the database.
Exploit the second-order SQL injection to leak the contents of the admin_notes table and retrieve the hidden flag.
hints: Registration and login use parameterized queries — direct SQL injection on those forms won't work. The vulnerability is second-order.

Your username is stored literally in the database. Later, when the dashboard loads, it's interpolated directly into SQL queries like: SELECT * FROM pets WHERE owner_username = '<your_stored_username>'
Try registering with a username like: ' UNION SELECT id,title,content,author,'','','','' FROM admin_notes-- (adjust column count to match the target query's columns)

The pets query returns 8 columns: id, name, species, breed, age, weight, owner_username, notes, microchip. Your UNION SELECT needs to match. Try: ' UNION SELECT id,title,content,'Note',1,1,author,created_date,'' FROM admin_notes-- and check the dashboard for leaked notes appearing as 'pets'.

title: NovusBank — Blind Boolean SQL Injection
description: NovusBank is an online banking portal with accounts, transfers, statements, and a transaction search feature. The search functionality appears harmless — it only tells you whether transactions were found or not. But beneath the surface, the query construction has a critical flaw. A hidden database table holds a secret that can only be extracted one character at a time through careful boolean-based blind SQL injection. Can you extract the flag without ever seeing the raw query output?
objectives: Log in using one of the provided demo accounts and explore the banking portal.
Identify the vulnerable input in the Transaction Search feature.
Confirm boolean-based blind SQL injection by crafting payloads that produce different responses (found vs. not found).
Extract the flag from the 'flags' table character by character using SUBSTRING-based boolean conditions.
Reconstruct the full flag in the format TARANTU{32-hex-characters}.
hints: The Transaction Search page at /search takes a query parameter 'q' and builds a SQL query using string concatenation. Try injecting a single quote to see how the app behaves.

The page shows '✓ transactions found' or '✗ No transactions found' — this binary response is your oracle for blind injection. Try: ' OR 1=1 -- to see if you get results, then ' AND 1=2 -- to confirm the difference.
There is a table called 'flags' with a column called 'secret'. Use a payload like: ' OR SUBSTRING((SELECT secret FROM flags LIMIT 1),1,7)='TARANTU' -- to confirm the flag table exists.

Extract the flag one character at a time. The flag format is TARANTU{<32 hex chars>}, so test positions 9 through 40 against characters 0-9 and a-f. Example: ' OR SUBSTRING((SELECT secret FROM flags LIMIT 1),9,1)='a' -- . Automate this with a script in the browser console using fetch() for efficiency.

title: NexaBank — JWT Forgery & Blind SQLi Chain
description: NexaBank is an online banking portal with accounts, transfers, statements, and an auditor compliance dashboard. The system uses JWT-based authentication with three roles: user, auditor, and admin. The admin panel requires a separate password that cannot be cracked. However, the auditor dashboard — accessible only to auditor-role users — contains a subtle vulnerability. Can you chain multiple weaknesses to extract the system's most closely guarded secret?
objectives: Log in with a demo account and explore the banking portal.
Examine the Session Info page to understand the JWT structure and signing algorithm.
Crack the weak HMAC secret used to sign JWTs and forge a token with the auditor role.
Access the auditor dashboard and exploit the account search feature to extract the flag from the vault_secrets table using blind boolean SQL injection.
hints: Visit the Session Info page — it shows your full JWT token, the decoded header (HS256), and payload. The token is in the nxb_token cookie.
The JWT HMAC secret is a very common word. Try tools like hashcat or jwt_tool with a common wordlist. The secret is one of the most obvious passwords people use.
Once you crack the secret, forge a new JWT with role set to 'auditor' and replace your nxb_token cookie. The auditor panel will then be accessible in the sidebar.
The auditor search field is vulnerable to SQL injection. It queries: WHERE username = '<your_input>'. Use boolean-based blind injection with UNION or OR conditions.

The flag is in the vault_secrets table, column key_value, where key_name = 'system_flag'. Extract it character by character: try ' OR (SELECT SUBSTR(key_value,1,1) FROM vault_secrets WHERE key_name='system_flag')='T' -- and check if the response says 'Account Found' (true) or 'No Account Found' (false).
Automate extraction: iterate position 1..N and for each position test characters A-Z, a-z, 0-9, {, }, _ until you find a match (Account Found response). Build the flag character by character.

title: HorizonCorp — SSRF to SQL Injection Chain
description: HorizonCorp is a corporate employee portal with an employee directory, HR documents, performance reviews, and a handy link preview tool for quick research. Beneath the polished surface, an internal microservice endpoint exists that was never meant to be accessed by users. Can you chain two vulnerabilities together to extract classified records from the hidden database?
objectives: Log in and explore the portal features, paying close attention to page source code.
Discover the hidden internal API endpoint that is not linked in the UI.
Use the Link Preview tool to reach the internal endpoint via SSRF.
Exploit a SQL injection vulnerability on the internal endpoint to extract the flag from a hidden database table using UNION SELECT.
hints: View the page source of any authenticated page carefully — look for HTML comments that reference internal endpoints.
The HTML source contains a comment mentioning /internal/employee-search?q=<name>. Try accessing it directly in the browser — it will be blocked.

The Link Preview tool makes server-side requests. Try using it to access http://localhost:3000/internal/employee-search?q=Alice — the SSRF bypasses the Referer header check because server-side requests don't include it.
The 'q' parameter is vulnerable to SQL injection. Use UNION SELECT to discover tables: try q=' UNION SELECT 1,sql,3,4,5,6 FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table'--
Once you find the classified_records table, extract the flag: q=' UNION SELECT 1,content,3,4,5,6 FROM classified_records WHERE record_type='FLAG'--

title: NexusArena — XSS to IDOR Exploit Chain
description: NexusArena is a competitive gaming platform with player profiles, leaderboards, arena chat, and an admin panel. The platform has multiple vulnerabilities that individually seem minor, but chaining them together grants access to classified data. A hidden player account holds a mythic item with a secret — can you steal the admin's session and enumerate your way to the flag?
objectives: Create or use an existing player account and explore the platform features.
Discover that the Arena Chat does not sanitize HTML in messages — craft a stored XSS payload.
Use the XSS to steal the admin bot's session cookie via the webhook collector endpoint.
Trigger the admin bot to visit the chat and process your malicious message.
Use the stolen admin session cookie to access the admin panel.
Discover the IDOR vulnerability in the admin API and enumerate player IDs to find a hidden player whose inventory contains the flag.
hints: The Arena Chat renders message content as raw HTML — try posting a message with to execute JavaScript.
The webhook collector endpoint at /webhook/collect?data=YOUR_DATA logs anything you send. Use this as your exfiltration target for the admin cookie.
Craft an XSS payload like: — then visit /bot/visit to trigger the admin bot.
After the bot visits, check the webhook log for the admin's nexus_session cookie value. Set it in your browser with: document.cookie='nexus_session=STOLEN_VALUE'
The admin panel lists players starting from ID 2, but the API /admin/api/players/:id/inventory has no restriction on which ID you query. Try ID 1.
Player ID 1 is a hidden account called 'Phantom Zero' whose inventory contains a Mythic rarity item — the flag is in that item's description field.

title: Meridian Health — JWT Forgery & IDOR Chain
description: Meridian Health is a hospital's internal patient portal used by doctors, nurses, and researchers to manage medical records. The system uses JWT tokens for authentication with role-based access control. A research panel exists for authorized researchers to query patient records, and a separate admin panel requires OTP verification. Can you chain two vulnerabilities to access a classified patient record that isn't visible in the UI?
objectives: Log into the portal with the provided staff credentials and explore the application.
Examine your JWT session token and understand how authentication and role-based access works.
Escalate your privileges to access the research panel by forging a valid JWT with an elevated role.
Use the research panel's API to discover and access a hidden patient's classified medical record containing the flag.
hints: Visit the /session page to see your full JWT token. Notice the payload contains your role. The header tells you the signing algorithm is HS256.
HS256 JWTs are signed with a secret key. If the secret is a common word, you can crack it using tools like hashcat or jwt-cracker. Try common passwords.
Once you know the secret, forge a new JWT with the role set to 'researcher'. Set it as the mh_token cookie. The Research Panel link will appear in the sidebar.
The research panel lists patients starting from ID 2. But the API endpoint /api/patients/{id}/records accepts any ID. Try ID 1 — there's a hidden test patient whose records contain the flag.
The admin panel requires an OTP code that cannot be guessed — it's a red herring. The researcher role is sufficient to access the records API.

title: NeonMart — XSS to SQLi Chain Attack
description: NeonMart is a premium e-commerce platform with products, reviews, and an admin inventory panel. A pair of vulnerabilities lurk beneath the polished surface — one in user-generated content and another in the admin's search functionality. Neither alone is enough. Chain them together to extract the classified flag from the supplier contracts database.
objectives: Create a customer account and explore the store, products, and review system.
Discover and exploit a stored XSS vulnerability to steal the admin's session cookie.
Use the stolen admin session to access the admin inventory panel.
Find and exploit a SQL injection vulnerability in the admin search to extract the flag from the supplier_contracts table.
hints: Product reviews accept user input. Does the review comment get properly sanitized when displayed? Try submitting a review with HTML tags or an tag.
There is a webhook collector at /webhook/collect?data=... and a viewer at /webhook. You can use these to receive exfiltrated data. Craft an XSS payload like:
After posting your XSS payload as a review, trigger the admin bot by visiting /bot/visit. Then check /webhook to see if the admin's session cookie was captured.

Set the stolen admin session cookie (nm_session=...) in your browser, then visit /admin/inventory. The search feature concatenates input directly into SQL. Try: ' UNION SELECT 1,2,3,4,5,6,contract_details FROM supplier_contracts--

title: HireScope
description: HireScope is a modern job board platform built for mid-size tech companies to post positions and manage candidate pipelines. Employers create listings, candidates submit applications with confidential documents (resumes, cover letters, salary expectations), and recruiters review everything from a unified dashboard. The platform prides itself on keeping candidate data private and secure — or so they claim.
objectives: Create or use an account on the platform and explore the features available to your role.
Find a way to access documents that belong to other users on the platform.
Discover confidential information stored in a document you should not be able to view.
Retrieve the hidden flag from the platform's data.
hints: The platform has an API endpoint for retrieving documents. Pay attention to how document IDs work in the URLs.
When you view your own documents, notice the download link format — it includes a numeric document ID. What happens if you change that number?
The /api/documents/:id endpoint returns document content as JSON. Try incrementing the ID to access documents belonging to other candidates.
There is a confidential notes document with ID 7 that belongs to another user. Fetch /api/documents/7 while logged in to see its contents — the flag is embedded in the document text.

title: NexusLink — Server-Side Request Forgery
description: NexusLink is a team collaboration portal where employees share links across project channels. The Link Preview feature fetches URLs server-side to generate previews — but what happens when you point it at something it shouldn't be able to reach?
objectives: Log in and explore the collaboration portal and its features.
Find a feature that makes the server fetch external content on your behalf.
Discover an internal API endpoint that is blocked from direct access.
Trick the server into fetching the restricted endpoint and retrieve the flag.
hints: The "Share a Link" feature in any channel sends URLs to the server, which fetches them and returns a preview. What if the URL pointed to the server itself?
Check the API Documentation page — it lists some endpoints marked as "internal only." These return 403 when you access them directly.
The internal endpoints block requests that have an Origin or Referer header (which browsers always send). But the server's own fetch() calls don't include those headers.
In any channel's Share a Link form, enter: http://localhost:3000/internal/devops/credentials — the server fetches itself, bypasses the external check, and the flag is in the docker_token field.

title: DeskFlow — Helpdesk Under Pressure
description: DeskFlow is an internal IT helpdesk portal used by support agents to manage tickets, track escalations, and leave internal notes. The ticket search feature connects directly to the database — and when something goes wrong, the system is a little too forthcoming about why.
objectives: Log in to the helpdesk portal using one of the provided demo accounts.
Use the ticket search feature to trigger a database error that reveals information about the underlying schema.
Identify a hidden table and column that is not visible anywhere in the normal application interface.
Craft a query through the search form that extracts the sensitive value stored in that table.
hints: The search box passes your input directly into a query. What happens when the SQL becomes invalid — does the app stay silent?
Try injecting a single quote into the search field. Study what the error message reveals about the query structure and database schema.
SQL errors from sqlite often mention table names. Once you know the schema, try using UNION SELECT to pull data from a table that isn't shown in the normal interface.

The hidden table is called sys_config and has columns config_key and config_value. Try: ' UNION SELECT config_key,config_value,config_value,config_value,config_value,config_value,config_value FROM sys_config--

title: Arcadia Library — Overdue Injection
description: Arcadia Library has launched a modern member portal for browsing its catalog, managing loans, and making reservations. The development team recently overhauled the search feature for better performance — but something in their implementation may have opened an unexpected door.
objectives: Log in to the Arcadia Library member portal and explore its features.
Identify a part of the catalog interface that handles input in an unusual way.
Craft an input that causes the page to execute code of your choosing.
Use that execution to read the flag from its storage location and display it on the page.
hints: The library catalog has a search feature. Pay close attention to what happens in the browser when you perform a search — not just what the server returns.
Open the browser developer tools and look at the page source for the catalog route. Notice how the search term is displayed back to you after a search.
The search term is read from the URL using URLSearchParams and inserted into the DOM. Find which JavaScript method is used for that insertion — some are safer than others.
The search term is placed into the DOM with innerHTML, making it executable. The flag is stored in a cookie. Try a payload like:

title: Oakridge Public Library — The Dewey Deception
description: Oakridge Public Library uses token-based access to protect member documents. Each member receives a unique access token for retrieving their personal notes. But are these tokens truly unpredictable, or can you figure out how to read someone else's private records?
objectives: Log in to the library portal and explore your member account features.
Examine your own access token and understand how it might be constructed.
Find a way to access member notes belonging to a staff account you don't have credentials for.
Retrieve the hidden flag from a privileged user's private documents.
hints: Your member access token is displayed in several places. Take a close look at its format — does it remind you of any common encoding schemes?
Try decoding your own token using Base64. Notice the pattern in the decoded value and think about what other values might follow that same pattern.
The tokens follow the format base64("member_N") where N is a user ID. There are more users in the system than the three demo accounts shown on the login page.
The staff account is user ID 4. Compute its token: base64("member_4") = "bWVtYmVyXzQ=". Enter this token on the Member Notes page to retrieve the flag.

title: SkyVoyage
description: SkyVoyage is a next-generation travel booking platform launched by a boutique travel-tech startup in Austin, TX. They aggregate flights, hotels, and rental cars into seamless itineraries while rewarding loyal travelers with VoyagePoints redeemable for upgrades and discounts. The platform has been rapidly onboarding beta users and recently launched a loyalty dashboard for its growing community.
objectives: Create an account and explore the platform to understand how user profiles work.
Find a way to gain elevated privileges that your account should not have.
Access a restricted section of the platform that is not visible to regular users.
Retrieve the hidden flag from the restricted area.
hints: After registering, check your dashboard carefully — what information is displayed about your account that might correspond to database fields?
The registration form only shows a few fields, but what happens if you send additional data in the POST request body?
There is a role field stored in the database for each user. The server doesn't filter which fields it accepts during registration — this is called mass assignment.

Intercept or craft a POST request to /register with an extra field: "role" set to "admin". Use browser dev tools, curl, or modify the form to include this field. Then log in and look for new navigation options.

title: GovPortal — JWT 'none' Algorithm Bypass
description: GovPortal is a government citizen services portal used by staff to manage permits, tax records, and official documents. The portal uses JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) for authentication. A classified records section is restricted to administrators only. Can you bypass the authentication to access the classified documents?
objectives: Log in to the portal using the provided demo credentials and explore the application.
Navigate to your profile page to examine your JWT session token and understand its structure.
Identify the vulnerability in how the server validates JWT tokens.
Forge a modified JWT to escalate your privileges and access the classified records page to retrieve the flag.
hints: Visit the 'My Profile' page — your full JWT is displayed there along with its decoded header and payload. Notice the 'alg' field in the header and the 'role' field in the payload.
The server's JWT verification has a critical flaw: it accepts tokens where the algorithm is set to 'none', meaning no signature verification is performed.

To exploit this: decode your JWT's header and payload from base64url, change the header's 'alg' to 'none' and the payload's 'role' to 'admin', then re-encode them as base64url. The final token format should be: <header>.<payload>. (with a trailing dot and no signature).

In your browser console, try: let h = btoa(JSON.stringify({alg:'none',typ:'JWT'})).replace(/=/g,''); let p = btoa(JSON.stringify({sub:2,username:'sarah.permits',name:'Sarah Williams',role:'admin',department:'Permits & Licensing',iat:Math.floor(Date.now()/1000),exp:Math.floor(Date.now()/1000)+86400})).replace(/=/g,'').replace(/\+/g,'-').replace(/\//g,'_'); document.cookie='gsp_token='+h+'.'+p+'.'; Then navigate to /admin/classified.

title: NexusArena — JWT Weak Secret Exploit
description: NexusArena is a competitive gaming platform with player profiles, leaderboards, and an admin panel. The platform uses JWT (JSON Web Tokens) for authentication — but how strong is the signing secret? Capture your session token, crack the HMAC key, and forge yourself an admin identity to access the restricted panel.
objectives: Log in with one of the demo player accounts and explore the platform.
Navigate to the Session Info page to inspect your JWT token and understand its structure.
Extract the JWT and attempt to crack the HS256 HMAC secret using common wordlists or known weak passwords.
Forge a new JWT with the 'admin' role and replace your session cookie to access the Admin Panel and retrieve the flag.
hints: The Session Info page shows you the raw JWT and its decoded header/payload. Notice the algorithm is HS256 — this means the token is signed with a shared secret key.
Copy your JWT from the Session Info page or from the 'nexus_token' cookie. Try cracking the HMAC secret with tools like hashcat, jwt_tool, or even a simple script that tests common passwords.
The HMAC signing secret is an extremely common word. Think of the most obvious passwords people use — 'password', 'secret', '123456', 'key', etc.

Once you know the secret, use a tool like jwt.io or a script to create a new token where the 'role' field is set to 'admin'. Set the forged token as your 'nexus_token' cookie in the browser console: document.cookie = 'nexus_token=<forged_token>'; then navigate to /admin.

title: HelpdeskPro — Network Diagnostics Escape
description: HelpdeskPro is an IT helpdesk platform with built-in network diagnostic tools. The diagnostics panel lets agents run ping, nslookup, and traceroute against target hosts — but the input filtering might not cover every trick in the book.
objectives: Log into the helpdesk system and explore the available features.
Find the tool that interacts with the underlying system and test its input handling.
Discover a way to bypass the input restrictions and run unintended operations.
Extract the hidden secret stored on the server.
hints: The diagnostics page warns about blocked characters. What characters are NOT blocked?
There are multiple ways to chain or embed commands in a shell besides semicolons and pipes — think about how shells handle substitution.
Command substitution with $() or backticks allows embedding one command inside another. The filter does not block these.
In the target field, enter: $(cat .flag) — the shell substitution will execute `cat .flag` and embed the flag value into the output of the outer command.

title: NexusBank — Hidden Ledger
description: NexusBank's digital portal lets customers view statements, transfer funds, and manage beneficiaries. The transaction search feature appears functional and harmless — but something lurks beneath the surface. A separate internal table holds secrets that no ordinary customer should ever reach.
objectives: Log in to the banking portal using one of the provided demo accounts.
Locate a feature that passes user input into a database query.
Determine how many columns the underlying query returns.
Extract the contents of a hidden database table to retrieve the flag.
hints: The transaction search bar sends your input directly into a query. What happens when you put unusual characters in the search field?
A database error message reveals the structure of the query. You need your injected SELECT to return the same number of columns as the original query.
Try appending something like ' UNION SELECT 1,2,3,4,5,6-- to the search input and adjust the column count until you stop getting errors.
Once column count is confirmed, replace one of the numeric placeholders with secret_value and target the vault_secrets table: ' UNION SELECT 1,secret_value,3,4,5,6 FROM vault_secrets--

title: NexusBank — Multi-Chain Exploitation
description: NexusBank is an online banking portal with accounts, transfers, statements, referral rewards, and internal audit systems. No single vulnerability is critical — but chaining five low-to-medium severity issues together leads to full compromise of classified records. You must escalate from a standard user account all the way through the internal audit system to extract the flag.
objectives: Create a standard banking account and explore the portal's features including the referral program.
Exploit a business logic flaw in the referral system to gain premium tier access.
Use a stored XSS vulnerability in premium research notes to steal a compliance officer's session.
Crack the weak JWT secret from the compliance session and forge an auditor token.
Use the auditor's URL verification tool to perform SSRF against the internal audit API.
Extract the flag from the classified_records table via blind boolean SQL injection through the SSRF, bypassing the WAF.
hints: The referral redemption endpoint doesn't properly enforce one-use-per-code. Try submitting the same referral code rapidly multiple times — each request grants 25 points regardless of prior redemptions.
Premium users can post research notes with 'HTML formatting supported'. The server strips
Use /webhook/collect?data=... as your exfiltration endpoint and /webhook to view captured data. The bot simulates XSS execution when it detects event handlers pointing to the webhook.

Once you have the compliance officer's JWT from the webhook, visit /session to examine it. The JWT uses HS256 with a very common secret word — try cracking it with tools like jwt.io or hashcat with a common wordlist.

Forge a new JWT with role set to 'auditor' to access the Audit Dashboard. The URL verification tool fetches URLs server-side — try http://localhost:3000/internal/audit/search?q=LOGIN to access the internal API via SSRF.

The internal audit search endpoint is vulnerable to SQL injection but has a WAF that blocks uppercase SQL keywords. Use case variations like 'uNiOn' and 'sElEcT' to bypass. The flag is in the classified_records table (columns: record_key, record_value).

For blind boolean extraction: craft queries like: ' OR 1=1 -- to confirm injection, then use SUBSTR on (sElEcT record_value FROM classified_records WHERE record_key='flag') character by character, checking if the response shows 'Found' vs 'No records found'.
Check /robots.txt and /api/debug/config for interesting information — but beware, not everything you find is what it seems.

title: NexusArena — Multi-Chain Exploitation
description: NexusArena is a competitive gaming platform with player profiles, leaderboards, community forums, and admin tools. Beneath the surface, a chain of vulnerabilities connects user-generated content to the platform's most classified data. No single flaw is enough — you must chain four distinct exploits to reach the flag hidden deep in the internal database.
objectives: Exploit a stored XSS vulnerability in the forum to steal a moderator's session cookie.
Analyze the stolen JWT token and crack the weak HMAC secret to forge an admin session.
Use the admin server-check tool to perform SSRF and access internal API endpoints.
Exploit a SQL injection vulnerability in the internal leaderboard API to extract the flag from the classified_data table.
hints: The forum strips

After stealing the moderator's JWT from the webhook page, decode it and notice it uses HS256. The HMAC secret is a very common word. Try cracking it with tools like jwt_tool, hashcat, or jwt.io. Then forge a new token with role set to 'admin'.

The admin dashboard has a Server Check tool that fetches URLs server-side. The /internal/api/* endpoints are blocked from direct access but reachable from localhost. Try fetching http://localhost:3000/internal/api/stats to discover table names.

The internal leaderboard API at /internal/api/leaderboard?q=... concatenates user input directly into SQL. Use UNION SELECT to extract data from the classified_data table: ' UNION SELECT 1,entry_value,3,4,5,6,7,8 FROM classified_data WHERE entry_key='system_flag' --

title: SkyWard Travel — Multi-Chain Exploitation
description: SkyWard Travel is a premium flight and hotel booking platform with a loyalty rewards system, VIP tiers, and an internal staff dashboard. No single vulnerability is critical on its own — but chaining three distinct flaws together will give you access to a classified booking record containing the flag. Can you go from a regular traveler to accessing top-secret internal data?
objectives: Create an account and explore the loyalty/promo system to find a business logic flaw that grants VIP tier access without legitimately earning enough points.
Use your VIP access to obtain your session token, then crack the JWT signing secret and forge a staff-level token.
Access the staff booking dashboard and exploit an IDOR vulnerability in the booking detail API to find a hidden classified booking containing the flag.
hints: The API docs mention a batch promo endpoint at /api/loyalty/apply-promos that accepts an array of codes. What happens if you send the same code multiple times in one request? The per-code dedup check has a logic flaw when processing duplicates within the same batch.

Once you're VIP, the Session Info page shows your full JWT token and reveals the algorithm is HS256. The HMAC secret is a very common word — try cracking it with tools like jwt_tool, hashcat, or by manually testing common secrets.
Forge a new JWT with the role changed to 'staff' and set it as your sw_token cookie. This unlocks the Staff Dashboard with its booking management features.

The staff dashboard shows bookings starting from BK-1050, but the booking API at /api/staff/bookings/:id doesn't restrict which IDs you can query. Try lower booking IDs like BK-0001 — there might be a hidden classified booking not shown in the UI.
